1.text: Module docstring: this lab implements Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch, then compares character-level and BPE tokenization with a simple bigram model.

17.text: This lab is mostly about counting and merging, but we still need a little math later to measure whether the learned subwords actually help the language model.

18.text: BPE learns entirely from the corpus itself, so the first step is simply to find the raw names file we will mine for repeated character patterns.

19.text: The merge history depends on corpus statistics, so we keep the setup stable enough that the same subwords emerge from run to run.

21.text: BPE is a cumulative greedy process: early merges shape every later token, so reproducibility matters more than it first appears.

26.text: Locate the shared names corpus. Every later merge rule will be discovered from these strings alone, with no hand-written vocabulary.

27.text: Fetch the corpus if needed so the tokenizer can be learned from scratch with no manual setup.

28.text: Only import the downloader if the file is missing. The real story starts once we begin counting recurring fragments.

30.text: A tiny but realistic corpus: enough repeated fragments for subwords like an, er, and ia to emerge naturally.

31.text: Fetch the file from the internet once and save it locally. After this first download, every future run loads instantly from disk.

33.text: Load every name, strip whitespace, skip blanks. Each name becomes one "document" for training.

34.text: Shuffle so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical order.

35.text: A quick sanity check that we loaded the full dataset. With ~32K names, we have plenty of data to discover meaningful merge patterns.

40.text: Every unique character gets an integer id. BOS marks the start and end of each sequence — the model learns when to stop generating.

41.text: Collect every unique character (a-z), this becomes the base vocabulary, same as lab 01.

42.text: Character-to-integer mapping. In labs 01-08, this WAS the tokenizer. Now it's the baseline we'll beat.

43.text: Reverse mapping for decoding back to text.

44.text: BOS (beginning/end of sequence) gets the next available id, just past the last character.

45.text: 27 tokens total: 26 lowercase letters plus one special BOS token. This tiny vocabulary is the starting point for both tokenizers.

47.text: Print a section header. The character tokenizer is the baseline that BPE will be measured against.

50.text: Show the vocabulary breakdown: how many real characters vs control tokens.

53.text: Encode a name to character tokens: wrap with BOS on both ends, look up each character's integer ID. This is the baseline that BPE will improve upon.

54.text: Docstring for the character encoder function.

55.text: BOS + character ids + BOS. Identical to how labs 01-08 encode, just factored into a reusable function.

58.text: Reverse the encoding: map each token ID back to its character, skipping the BOS markers.

59.text: Docstring for the character decoder function.

60.text: Skip BOS tokens, map ids back to characters, join into a string.

66.text: BPE starts with the character vocabulary and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token.

67.text: This is exactly how GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, and LLaMA build their tokenizers — at much larger scale with ~50K merges.

70.text: The fundamental tradeoff: larger vocab means shorter sequences (faster) but more embedding parameters.

74.text: Print a section header for the BPE tokenizer training phase.

78.text: 50 merges is tiny, GPT-2 uses 50,000. But even 50 will noticeably compress our names corpus.

80.text: Set how many new merge rules BPE will learn. This is the knob that trades vocabulary growth for shorter sequences.

81.text: This corpus starts at the character level. Each sequence is still just BOS, characters, and BOS before any merges are learned.

83.text: Reuse the same BOS id so char and BPE tokenizers are comparable.

84.text: 27 base tokens before any merges happen.

86.text: Now turn each name into a list of token ids so the merge loop can operate on integer sequences rather than raw strings.

87.text: Comment: build the initial corpus by wrapping each name with BOS and converting to integer IDs.

88.text: The corpus starts as character-level token sequences, identical to what the char tokenizer produces.

89.text: Build the initial corpus for BPE training: each name becomes a list of integer token IDs, starting at character granularity.

90.text: Wrap with BOS, convert characters to integer ids. This is the starting point BPE will compress.

91.text: Accumulate each tokenized name into the corpus list. This gives us the complete training set that BPE will iteratively compress through merges.

94.text: Once the corpus is integerized, the next step is counting adjacent pairs. That pair-frequency table drives the whole tokenizer.

95.text: Docstring: count how often each adjacent token pair appears across the entire corpus.

96.text: Simple dictionary to accumulate pair frequencies.

97.text: Scan every tokenized name in the corpus.

98.text: Slide a window of size 2 across each sequence.

99.text: Each pair is a tuple of two adjacent token ids.

100.text: Increment the count. The pair with the highest count will be merged next.

101.text: Return the complete frequency dictionary. The pair with the highest count will be merged next.

104.text: The core merge operation: scan every sequence left-to-right, replacing adjacent tokens matching the pair with the new merged ID. This is greedy — the leftmost match wins.

105.text: Docstring for merge_pair: replace every occurrence of a given pair with a new single token ID.

106.text: Allocate a fresh corpus list that will hold the post-merge versions of every sequence. We rebuild rather than mutate in place to keep the logic clean.

108.text: Walk through the old sequence building a new one token by token, replacing any occurrence of the target pair with the merged token along the way.

109.text: Manual index, we sometimes skip ahead by 2 when we find the pair.

110.text: Walk through the sequence looking for the target pair.

111.text: Pattern matching: if the current token and the next match our target pair, emit the merged token and skip ahead by two positions.

112.text: Replace the pair with the single merged token, this is where compression happens.

113.text: Skip past both original tokens since we consumed them as one.

114.text: Else branch: no pair match at this position. Fall through to copy the current token unchanged.

115.text: No match here, keep the original token.

116.text: Move the scan pointer forward by one position. We only skip by two when we find and merge the target pair; otherwise we advance normally.

117.text: This name is done, add its merged sequence to the new corpus.

118.text: Return the fully merged corpus. Every sequence is now shorter by the number of merges that fired.

121.text: Step 2 comment: iteratively merge the most frequent pair. Each merge creates a new token and shrinks all sequences.

123.text: The merge table records what was merged — it IS the tokenizer, replayed during encoding.

124.text: The merge table: maps each learned pair to its new token id. This IS the tokenizer.

125.text: Tracks the growing vocabulary size as merges create new tokens.

128.text: Decode one token id by recursively unpacking merge history until it reaches base characters.

129.text: Docstring: decode a single BPE token ID to its string representation, recursively expanding merges.

130.text: BOS decodes to empty string, it's a control token, not a character.

131.text: BOS decodes to empty string. It's a control token with no visible text representation.

132.text: Base characters decode directly via the character mapping.

133.text: Base characters (a-z) decode directly from the id-to-character lookup table.

135.text: For merged tokens, find which pair was merged and recursively decode both halves.

136.text: Linear scan through merges, fine for 50 merges, would need a reverse map at scale.

137.text: Recursive call: a merged token might itself be composed of merged tokens.

138.text: Fallback for unknown token IDs — should never happen in practice.

141.text: Human-friendly token display for the training log.

142.text: Docstring: like bpe_decode_token but keeps BOS visible as '|' for human-readable output.

143.text: Show BOS as a readable tag.

147.text: Merged tokens show their full decoded string (e.g., token 28 might display as "an").

148.text: For merged tokens, recursively expand both components into their display form.

149.text: Concatenate the display strings of both halves of the merge.

153.text: Helper function for the training log: convert any token ID to a human-readable string.

155.text: The main BPE training loop: 50 rounds of find-best-pair, merge, repeat.

156.text: Count all adjacent pairs in the current corpus.

157.text: Safety check, if every sequence is length 1, nothing left to merge.

158.text: Base characters display as themselves — single letter output.

159.text: Merged tokens use bpe_decode_token to recursively expand to their full string.

162.text: Announce the training loop. From here on, each iteration finds the most frequent pair and folds it into a new token.

163.text: Announce how many merge operations we will perform.

164.text: Record this merge in the table. During encoding, merges are replayed in this exact order.

165.text: Print dashes to separate the table header from the data rows.

167.text: Run the greedy merge loop. Earlier merges change the corpus seen by later merges, so the learned vocabulary is order-dependent.

168.text: Count all adjacent pairs in the current (possibly already-merged) corpus.

169.text: Safety check: if no pairs remain (all sequences are length 0 or 1), stop early.

170.text: Apply the merge to the entire corpus, every occurrence of the pair becomes one token.

171.text: Vocabulary grows by one with each merge.

173.text: Greedy selection: always merge the globally most frequent pair. Not optimal, but close — and fast.

174.text: Record the frequency of the best pair for the training log.

175.text: Assign a new token ID: simply the current vocabulary size, so IDs increment from 27 upward.

176.text: Record this merge in the table. The order matters: during encoding, merges are replayed in exactly this sequence.

178.text: Log the first 20 merges and every 10th after that, plus the very last one — so the output stays readable.

179.text: Build a human-readable string showing which tokens were merged (e.g., 'a'+'n').

180.text: Print one row of the training log: merge number, pair IDs, visual form, frequency, and new vocab size.

182.text: Apply the merge to every sequence in the corpus. The corpus shrinks because two tokens become one wherever this pair appeared.

183.text: Increment the vocabulary size. Every merge adds exactly one new token.

185.text: Print the final BPE vocabulary size: base tokens plus all learned merges.

188.text: BPE decoding just expands every token id back into its character sequence and drops the BOS markers.

189.text: Replay merges in training order. Earlier merges are higher priority (more frequent patterns).

190.text: Apply each merge to the token sequence. After all merges, common subwords are single tokens.

193.text: BPE encoding replays the learned merges in training order, which is why the merge history has to be stored explicitly.

196.text: Encoding with learned merges: start from characters, then replay every merge in training order. This greedy left-to-right strategy is how GPT-2/3/4 encode text.

199.text: Start with character-level tokens, exactly like the character encoder. BPE encoding then compresses from here.

200.text: A mix of short, long, common, and unusual names to show compression varies.

201.text: Apply merges in the order they were learned. Earlier merges captured the most frequent patterns, so they fire most often.

202.text: Apply each merge by calling merge_pair on a single-element list, then unwrap. The sequence gets shorter with each applicable merge.

203.text: Return the final compressed token sequence. Common subwords are now single tokens.

206.text: The next section switches from tokenizer construction to evaluation: how much shorter are sequences, and what does the new vocabulary look like?

208.text: For each example, encode with both tokenizers and show the compression ratio.

209.text: Character encoding: always len(name) + 2 tokens (one per char plus two BOS markers).

210.text: BPE encoding: fewer tokens because common patterns like "an", "er" are now single tokens.

211.text: Compression ratio, how many character tokens each BPE token replaces on average.

212.text: A mix of hand-picked and random names to show how BPE compression varies: short common names compress heavily, long unusual ones less so.

214.text: Add some random names from the dataset so the examples are not cherry-picked.

216.text: Deduplicate while preserving order and cap at 10 examples.

221.text: Loop through each example name, encoding it with both tokenizers for comparison.

222.text: Encode with the character tokenizer: always len(name) + 2 tokens (one per char plus two BOS).

223.text: Sum up character tokens across the entire corpus.

224.text: Sum up BPE tokens across the entire corpus, this is the real compression measurement.

225.text: Average tokens per name with character encoding.

226.text: Average tokens per name with BPE, expect roughly 1.3-1.5x shorter.

236.text: Count total tokens across the corpus for both tokenizers. This is the concrete compression win BPE is buying.

237.text: Encode every name with BPE and sum up all tokens across the corpus.

238.text: Average character tokens per name — the baseline measurement.

239.text: Average BPE tokens per name — should be notably less than the character average.

241.text: Print the total number of names in the dataset.

244.text: Print the total character tokens across the corpus — the 'before' number.

245.text: Print the total BPE tokens across the corpus — the 'after' number.

248.text: Display the full merge table, this IS the BPE vocabulary, showing exactly what patterns the algorithm discovered.

250.text: Show the components that were merged (which might themselves be merged tokens).

251.text: Print the learned merged tokens so the reader can inspect which subwords the greedy algorithm discovered.

256.text: Print the base tokens (a-z) that form the foundation of the vocabulary.

257.text: Show which token ID is assigned to the BOS marker.

261.text: Display every learned merge rule. Reading this table reveals what patterns the algorithm discovered: common bigrams first (like 'an', 'er'), then longer chunks.

262.text: Decode the merged token to show what string it represents (e.g., token 28 = 'an').

263.text: Decode the first component of the merge, replacing the pipe symbol with a readable BOS tag.

265.text: Print one row: the new token ID, its decoded string, and which two tokens were merged to create it.

269.text: A bigram model is the simplest possible language model: just a transition probability table. No neural network needed.

270.text: Comment: the bigram model is P(next_token | current_token), just a lookup table of transition counts.

272.text: 2D count matrix: counts[a][b] = how often token b follows token a.

275.text: Tally one more occurrence of this token-to-token transition. The row with the highest total count will later define the most probable next tokens.

279.text: Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing prevents zero probabilities for unseen pairs.

282.text: The final section treats each tokenizer as a modeling choice: train a bigram model on both and compare likelihood plus samples.

283.text: Docstring: train a bigram model by counting transitions, smoothing, and normalizing.

284.text: Generate a name by sampling one token at a time from the bigram distribution.

285.text: Initialize a 2D count matrix of zeros: rows are current tokens, columns are next tokens.

286.text: Start with BOS, the model will sample what typically follows the start marker.

288.text: Increment the count for this specific token-to-token transition.

289.text: Look up the probability distribution for the current (last) token.

290.text: Sample from the cumulative distribution using a uniform random number.

291.text: Process each row of counts to produce a row of probabilities.

292.text: Walk through tokens by probability until we pass the random threshold.

293.text: Laplace smoothing: add 1 to each count, then normalize. This ensures no probability is exactly zero.

294.text: This token was selected, the cumulative probability exceeded our random draw.

297.text: Ancestral sampling from the bigram: pick a random next token weighted by the transition probabilities, repeat until BOS (end) or max length.

298.text: Docstring: sample a sequence from the bigram model by repeatedly drawing next tokens.

299.text: Start the generated sequence with BOS — the model will sample what typically follows the start marker.

302.text: Average log-likelihood per token measures how well the model predicts the training data.

303.text: Draw a uniform random number to use for sampling from the probability distribution.

304.text: Accumulate total log-likelihood across all tokens.

305.text: Walk through tokens by probability until the cumulative sum exceeds the random threshold.

307.text: For each adjacent pair in each sequence, look up its probability.

308.text: The probability assigned to this actual transition, higher is better.

310.text: Log probability, the fundamental unit of information theory. Sums are easier than products.

311.text: Return the generated sequence once it terminates.

312.text: Average over all tokens gives a per-token measure, comparable across different vocab sizes.

315.text: Evaluate model quality: average log-probability the model assigns to the actual next token. Higher means the model predicts the data better.

316.text: Docstring: compute average log-likelihood per token to measure model quality.

317.text: Encode the entire corpus with the character tokenizer for bigram training.

318.text: Train the character-level bigram, just counting transitions in a 27x27 table.

319.text: Measure how well it models the data. Lower perplexity = better model.

320.text: For each adjacent pair in each sequence, look up the probability the model assigns to that transition.

323.text: Add the log of the transition probability. Log-space addition avoids floating-point underflow from multiplying many small numbers.

325.text: Return the average: total log-likelihood divided by number of tokens. This per-token measure is comparable across different vocab sizes.

328.text: First fit a baseline bigram over character tokens so the BPE bigram has something honest to compare against.

330.text: Train the character-level bigram on the full corpus. With only 27 tokens, each bigram step covers one character — short-range patterns only.

331.text: Train the character bigram: count transitions in a 27x27 table, smooth, and normalize.

332.text: Evaluate the character bigram: compute average log-likelihood on the training data.

333.text: Then fit the BPE bigram on the compressed sequences. The vocabulary is larger, but each step covers more text.

334.text: Print the average log-likelihood per token for the character bigram.

335.text: Perplexity: exp(-avg_ll). Lower is better. Intuitively, it's the effective number of equally-likely choices at each step.

339.text: Train the BPE bigram on the same corpus. With 227 tokens, each step covers a subword — so a single bigram transition can capture multi-character patterns like 'an' → 'na'.

340.text: Train the BPE bigram: count transitions in a vocab_size x vocab_size table.

341.text: Evaluate the BPE bigram on the BPE-encoded corpus.

342.text: Sample a complete name by repeatedly drawing from the transition table.

343.text: Decode character ids back to a readable string.

344.text: Print the BPE perplexity. Expect lower perplexity per token since each BPE token carries more information.

346.text: Generate from the BPE bigram, each step jumps through common letter patterns, so names sound a bit more natural.

348.text: Sample from the BPE bigram's 77x77 transition table.

349.text: Decode BPE tokens back to a string, merged tokens expand to multiple characters.

354.text: Generate names from the character bigram. Quality is limited because each step only sees one character of context.

355.text: Sample a complete name from the character bigram by drawing from the 27x27 transition table.

356.text: Decode the character token IDs back to a readable string.

359.text: Generate names from the BPE bigram. Even with the same simple bigram model, BPE tokens capture longer patterns, producing more plausible names.

360.text: Sample a complete name from the BPE bigram. Each step can jump through multi-character patterns.

361.text: Sample from the BPE bigram's larger transition table.

362.text: Decode BPE tokens back to a string — merged tokens expand to multiple characters automatically.

371.text: The punchline: BPE is the algorithm behind every modern LLM tokenizer. At GPT scale (50K-100K merges on internet text), it's essential to keep sequences manageable.

372.text: Print the compression summary: how many tokens before vs after BPE, and the compression ratio.

376.text: This is the algorithm behind GPT-2, LLaMA, and most modern LLM tokenizers. At GPT scale, BPE with 50K-100K merges is essential.
